d1s0.what: The city is literally named after these falls: the Big Sioux River drops ~30 m here over ledges of pink Sioux quartzite — rock so hard it was quarried to build half the town. The 1881 Queen Bee Mill ruins beside the falls were once one of the largest flour mills in the world; it went bust in two years because the river's flow was never as reliable as promised.
see: Five-story observation tower (free) — best overview of the falls
Queen Bee Mill ruins — walk right up to the 140-year-old quartzite walls
The falls light up after dark; the café in the old power house does decent ice cream
tips: 45 minutes is plenty — it's a leg-stretch, not a hike
Free parking at Falls Park lot; downtown Phillips Ave (5 min) for dinner afterwards
d1s0.fact: That pink stone underfoot is ~1.6 billion years old — you'll be standing on rock a third the age of the Earth before you've even reached the national parks.

d2s0.what: A Moorish-revival arena in the middle of the prairie, redecorated every single year since 1892 with murals made entirely of corn — about 325,000 ears, nailed on cob by cob by local crews. It started as a stunt to prove South Dakota's soil could grow anything, and simply never stopped.
see: This year's exterior murals — a new theme is designed annually by a local artist
Step inside (free): it's a working basketball arena and concert hall with corn chandeliers
The hallway photos of every mural design back to the 1890s
tips: 15 minutes is genuinely enough; it's a photo stop
Free street parking on Main St; there's a decent coffee shop across the street
d2s0.fact: Locals call it the World's Only Corn Palace, and nobody has ever bothered to dispute the claim.

d2s1.what: The Badlands are what 500,000 years of rain does to an ancient sea bed and river floodplain: a 100-km wall of striped spires eroding about 2.5 cm every year — one of the fastest-vanishing landscapes on Earth. The bands of color are time itself: each stripe is a different epoch of mud, ash and river sand, stacked over 75 million years.
see: Door Trail (1.2 km, boardwalk then open moonscape) — walk through 'the Door,' a break in the Wall
Notch Trail (2.4 km round trip) — climbs a log ladder to a ledge with a huge view over the White River valley; skip if heights bother you
Window Trail (100 m) — the lazy option, straight to a framed view
tips: Golden hour (6–8pm) turns the grey spires orange and pink — this is why you're here in the evening
Rattlesnakes are real here: stay on obvious paths, look before you step
No shade at all — bring water even for these short walks
Bighorn sheep often stand on the spires near the trailhead parking at dusk
d2s1.fact: The Lakota named it 'mako sica' — literally 'bad land' — and French trappers agreed: 'les mauvaises terres à traverser,' bad lands to cross. Everyone who ever saw it named it the same thing.

d2s2.what: The park's main visitor center, named after Ben Reifel, the first Lakota member of Congress. The Badlands are one of the richest fossil beds on the planet — this whole area was a subtropical floodplain 34 million years ago, crawling with three-toed horses, giant pig-like entelodonts and sabre-toothed nimravids.
see: The Fossil Preparation Lab — real paleontologists cleaning real fossils behind glass, happy to chat
The 5-minute film is actually good if you want A/C
Fossil exhibits: oreodonts (sheep-sized grazers) are the local celebrity — they're found nowhere else in such numbers
tips: Refill every water bottle here — it's the only reliable water in the park
If the entrance booth was unmanned when you came in, you can buy the parks pass here
d2s2.fact: Kids still find fossils in the park every summer — if you spot bone, photograph it and report it at this desk; several major finds started exactly that way.

d2s3.what: In 1931, pharmacist Ted Hustead and his wife Dorothy were going broke in a town of 300 people. Dorothy's idea: put up highway signs offering FREE ICE WATER. It worked so well that Wall Drug is now a 7,000 m² roadside empire that draws up to 20,000 people a day — and the ice water is still free.
see: The backyard: the 24-foot jackalope you can sit on, the animatronic T-rex that roars every 12 minutes, the Cowboy Orchestra
The Western art collection in the café — genuinely good paintings, weirdly
Hot cake-sized donuts, made fresh — the maple ones go fast
tips: Coffee is still 5 cents. That's not a typo
It's open till ~9pm in summer; also great for breakfast tomorrow before the drive to Rushmore
You've been seeing its billboards for 500 km — that's the point; there were once Wall Drug signs in Paris and Antarctica
d2s3.fact: During WWII, GIs painted 'Wall Drug, 9,758 miles' signs across Europe and the Pacific — free advertising that made a South Dakota drugstore world-famous.

d3s0.what: Four 18-metre faces dynamited out of a granite mountain between 1927 and 1941 by sculptor Gutzon Borglum and 400 workers — 90% of the 'carving' was actually done with dynamite, to within 10 cm of the final surface. Washington for founding, Jefferson for expansion, Lincoln for preservation, Roosevelt for the parks and the century America stepped onto the world stage.
see: Presidential Trail (1 km boardwalk loop) — gets you under the faces, much better than the main terrace
The Sculptor's Studio: Borglum's 1:12 plaster model shows the original plan — full torsos down to the waists; money and WWII stopped it
Avenue of Flags for the classic framed photo
tips: The faces look southeast — morning light is exactly right, and you'll beat the buses before 9am
Parking is $10 cash/card per vehicle (private concession — the parks pass does NOT cover it), but the ticket is valid all year
Not one worker died in 14 years of hanging off a cliff with dynamite — the safety record is part of the legend
Leaving south, take Iron Mountain Road (US-16A): its tunnels were deliberately aligned to frame Rushmore in your windshield
d3s0.fact: Behind Lincoln's head there's a sealed Hall of Records containing porcelain panels of the Constitution and Declaration — built so a civilization 10,000 years from now can figure out who the faces are.

d3s1.what: Custer State Park's 29-km Wildlife Loop crosses open prairie owned by one of the largest publicly-held bison herds on Earth — about 1,300 animals descended from a rescue herd of 36, bought in 1914 when fewer than 1,000 bison were left alive anywhere. This is the closest thing America has to a safari.
see: Bison herds — often blocking the road entirely; that's the experience, enjoy the jam
The 'begging burros': feral donkeys, descendants of pack animals, who will put their heads through your window looking for snacks
Pronghorn — the fastest land animal in the western hemisphere (88 km/h), prairie dog towns, wild turkeys
tips: Stay IN the truck near bison — they look like sofas and charge like freight trains
Mid-morning works, but the herds are most active early and near dusk
The unpaved side roads off the loop are fine for your pickup and often have the herds to themselves
Your parks pass does not cover Custer SP — it's a state park, ~$20 per vehicle for a 7-day pass
d3s1.fact: Every September the park holds a real buffalo roundup: cowboys on horseback drive all 1,300 bison into corrals for vaccination — one of the last true Old West spectacles.

d3s2.what: The Needles Highway (SD-87) was declared impossible to build when Governor Peter Norbeck sketched it in 1919 — 22 km threaded through granite spires, finished in 1922 using low-grade dynamite and stubbornness. The Needles themselves are 1.7-billion-year-old granite eroded into organ pipes.
see: Needles Eye Tunnel: 2.6 m wide — fold your mirrors, honk politely, and inch through
The Cathedral Spires viewpoint just south of the tunnel
Sylvan Lake (5 min north) — the prettiest lake in the Black Hills, worth a 20-min stroll if you have gas in the tank
tips: Drive it slowly and use the pull-outs — the road IS the attraction
Watch for mountain goats on the rocks around the tunnel — a small band lives there
RVs can't fit through the tunnel, which keeps this road blissfully quieter than Rushmore
d3s2.fact: Norbeck ordered the road built 'not for speed, but for scenery' and personally flagged the route on foot and horseback. Engineers wanted straight lines; he made them keep every curve.

d3s3.what: The world's largest mountain carving in progress: a 172-metre sculpture of Lakota war leader Crazy Horse, begun in 1948 by sculptor Korczak Ziolkowski at the invitation of Lakota elder Henry Standing Bear — 'so the white man will know the red man has great heroes also.' The 27-metre face was finished in 1998; work continues on the horse's head.
see: The mountain itself (viewable from the visitor complex ~1 km away)
The Indian Museum of North America — one of the best Native art collections anywhere
Korczak's studio-home and the 1:34 model showing the finished design
tips: Entry is per vehicle (~$35) and NOT covered by any pass — it's a private nonprofit that has twice refused federal funding on principle
If you're short on time or budget: there's a decent free view of the mountain from a pullout on US-16/385 south of the entrance
When finished, all four Rushmore heads would fit inside Crazy Horse's head
d3s3.fact: Korczak died in 1982; his wife Ruth and seven of their ten children carried on. The family still runs it — no completion date has ever been announced, and they like it that way.

d4s0.what: A 264-metre tower of fused volcanic columns punching out of the prairie — the neck of an ancient volcano whose softer surroundings eroded away, or (in the Kiowa telling) a tree stump raised by the Great Spirit to save seven sisters from a giant bear, whose claws scored the flutes down its sides. It became America's first national monument in 1906, personally proclaimed by Theodore Roosevelt.
see: Tower Trail: the 2-km paved loop under the columns — each hexagonal column is up to 2.5 m wide, the largest example of columnar jointing on Earth
Climbers on the cracks — spot them for scale and the tower suddenly doubles in size
Prayer cloths and bundles tied to trees near the trail — this is an active sacred site for more than 20 tribes; look, don't touch
The prairie dog town by the entrance road
tips: Covered by your parks pass
Allow ~1.5 h including the entrance road; the loop itself is 45 min
June is the voluntary climbing closure out of respect for tribal ceremonies — in July you'll likely see climbers again
d4s0.fact: This is the mothership from 'Close Encounters of the Third Kind' (1977) — Spielberg chose it because it already looked like it belonged to another world.

d4s1.what: US-14 over the Bighorns climbs to 2,800 m through Shell Canyon, whose walls expose almost the entire geologic history of North America. Shell Falls drops 37 m over granite that is 2.5 billion years old — among the oldest exposed rock on the continent.
see: The 5-minute paved overlook loop at the falls interpretive site
Layer-cake canyon walls on the drive up — you pass through 2.5 billion years in 30 km
tips: It's a 20-minute stop with restrooms — perfect leg-stretch before the descent to Cody
Watch the truck's brakes on the way down the west side; use engine braking
Temperature drops ~10°C at the top — a taste of Yellowstone nights
d4s1.fact: You'll cross Granite Pass at 2,743 m — higher than anywhere the trip has taken you yet, and you're still two days from the Tetons.

d4s2.what: Cody calls itself Rodeo Capital of the World and backs it up: the Nite Rodeo has run every summer night since 1938, the longest-running nightly rodeo anywhere. The town itself was founded in 1896 by Buffalo Bill Cody, the original global superstar — his Wild West show had already toured Europe twice before the town existed.
see: Bareback and saddle bronc riding — the classic events, first hour
Bull riding — always the finale, always worth staying for
The kids' 'calf scramble' mid-show: every child in the stands invited into the arena to chase ribbons off calves
tips: Be seated by 7:45pm; 'Buzzard's Roost' seats (west, above the chutes) are the connoisseur's pick — you see the animals loaded
Evenings run cool even in July — bring the fleece
If you're in town early tomorrow, the Buffalo Bill Center of the West (5 museums in one) is genuinely world-class — even 90 minutes helps you read Yellowstone better
d4s2.fact: Buffalo Bill performed for Queen Victoria, invented the concept of the touring arena show, and was probably the most famous person on Earth in 1900 — this town is his retirement project.
